question_title: Will the community prediction be higher than 27.00% on 2026-02-14 for the Metaculus question "Will the United States experience negative GDP growth during Q1, Q2, or Q3 2026?"?
summary: The CP for the underlying US negative GDP question is currently at 0.28, just above the 0.27 threshold. Over the past 2 weeks, the CP has oscillated between 0.27 and 0.28, with about 35-40% of readings strictly above 0.27. The most recent readings (Feb 3) show 0.28-0.29, suggesting a mild upward drift. With 778 forecasters, the base is very stable, unlikely to see dramatic movement. The current above-threshold position and recent upward trend give a moderate lean toward YES, but the frequent oscillation back to exactly 0.27 creates meaningful probability of NO at the Feb 14 snapshot. Probability: 62%.

factors.description: Current CP is 0.28, already above the 0.27 threshold. Starting position favors YES.
Recent trend (Feb 1-3) shows slight upward drift to 0.28-0.29, with last 3 readings all above threshold.
CP frequently oscillates to exactly 0.27 (55-60% of recent readings), which would resolve NO. High uncertainty about exact snapshot value.
778 forecasters provides very stable base — unlikely to see dramatic moves in either direction over 10 days.
Tariff uncertainty and economic headwinds provide mild fundamental upward pressure on recession probability, supporting CP staying at or above current level.

**Summary of key findings:**

This is a meta-prediction about whether the CP for the US negative GDP growth question will be strictly above 27% on Feb 14, 2026.

**Critical CP data:**
- **Current CP: 0.28** (above the 0.27 threshold)
- The CP has been oscillating between 0.27 and 0.28 for ~2 weeks
- 778 forecasters = very stable, low-volatility base
- In the last week: ~41% of readings above 0.27, ~56% exactly at 0.27, ~4% below
- Most recent 3 readings (Feb 3): 0.29, 0.28, 0.28 — all above threshold
- Recent trend shows slight upward drift

**Fundamentals:** Tariff uncertainty and the Q1 2025 negative GDP precedent (-0.6%) provide mild upward pressure on recession probability. Goldman Sachs is above-consensus on growth, but trade policy uncertainty persists. These factors mildly support the CP staying at or above current levels.

**Key tension:** The CP frequently sits exactly at 0.27 (which does NOT satisfy "higher than 27.00%"), making this genuinely uncertain. But the current position is above threshold and recent momentum is slightly upward.
